content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-4441              322-6444

August 3, 1983


Harold R. Densmore
City Attorney
P.O. Box 51
Newman, CA  95360

Re: Advice Letter No. A-83-183

Dear Mr. Densmore:

Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Thomas
Engstrom, the Police Chief of the City of Newman. The facts as
stated in your letter, are that Chief Engstrom is considering
running for a position on the governing board of the local
school district. His question is, if he is elected to serve on
the Board, will this create a conflict of interest?

The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act do not prevent Chief Engstrom from assuming a position on
the school board. Instead the Act affects his ability to make
certain governmental decisions.

Government Code Section 87100¹/ provides that no local
public official can make, or participate in the making of, a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know
that he has a financial interest. An official has a "financial
interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect²/ on:

________________

1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

2/ The term "material financial effect" is defined in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copy enclosed.

Harold R. Densmore
August 3, 1983
Page 2


          (a)  Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000.

          (b)  Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000.

          (c)  Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or more
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

          (d)  Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management.  (Section 87103.)

     In his capacity as Police Chief and school board member,
Chief Engstrom must determine, on a case-by-case basis, whether
he must disqualify himself from a certain governmental decision.

     The salary that Chief Engstrom receives for his position
with the Police Department will not result in a need for
disqualification.  Section 82030(b)(2) provides that salary
received from a local government agency does not constitute
"income."  Thus, the Police Department is not considered to be a
source of income to Chief Engstrom.

     If you have any questions about "incompatibility of office"
problems, these should directed to the Attorney General's
Office.  Our advice is based solely upon the provisions of the
Political Reform Act.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosure

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance          Administration          Executive/Legal          Enforcement          Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662              322-5660              322-5901              322-4441              322-6444

August 1, 1983

Harold R. Densmore
City Attorney
P.O. Box 51
Newman, CA  95360
                                                    Re: A-83-183

Dear Mr. Densmore:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

City of Newman
CITY HALL
P.O. BOX 787
NEWMAN, CALIFORNIA 95360
(209) 862-3725

July 27, 1983


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

Our Chief of Police, who is appointed by the City Manager after
review with the City Council, has contemplated being a candidate
for a position on the Governing Board of the Local School District.
In the event he should be elected to serve on that Board, would
there be a possible conflict of interest? Thank you.

Yours very truly,

HAROLD R. DENSMORE,
City Attorney
P. O. Box51
Newman, CA 95360

HRD;cc


cc: Leslie L. Doolittle, City Manager

sections.question: In the event he should be elected to serve on that Board, would there be a possible conflict of interest?
sections.conclusion: The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act do not prevent Chief Engstrom from assuming a position on the school board. Instead the Act affects his ability to make certain governmental decisions. In his capacity as Police Chief and school board member, Chief Engstrom must determine, on a case-by-case basis, whether he must disqualify himself from a certain governmental decision. The salary that Chief Engstrom receives for his position with the Police Department will not result in a need for disqualification.
sections.question_synthetic: Would a Police Chief serving on a local school board governing body create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit Chief Engstrom from serving on the school board, but he must disqualify himself on a case-by-case basis from governmental decisions where he has a financial interest as defined by Government Code Section 87100. His Police Chief salary is not considered income and therefore does not create a conflict.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Includes detailed statutory analysis citing Government Code Sections 87100, 87103, and 82030. Document contains incoming request letter from City Attorney followed by FPPC response dated August 3, 1983.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In the event he should be elected to serve on that Board, would there be a possible conflict of interest?

CONCLUSION: The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act do not prevent Chief Engstrom from assuming a position on the school board. Instead the Act affects his ability to make certain governmental decisions. In his capacity as Police Chief and school board member, Chief Engstrom must determine, on a case-by-case basis, whether he must disqualify himself from a certain governmental decision. The salary that Chief Engstrom receives for his position with the Police Department will not result in a need for disqualification.

In his capacity as Police Chief and school board member, Chief Engstrom must determine, on a case-by-case basis, whether he must disqualify himself from a certain governmental decision. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a Police Chief may serve on a school board governing body without violating the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions, though he must disqualify himself from decisions involving his financial interests on a case-by-case basis.
